questions.question: Why is a partition key usually more important than broker brand in queue design?
options: Because it determines ordering boundaries, skew risk, and scaling behavior.
Because brokers never store messages on disk.
Because a partition key removes the need for acknowledgements.
Because all queues automatically rebalance hot keys evenly.
questions.explanation: Key choice shapes the system's core behavior.

questions.question: What problem does a visibility timeout or delivery lease primarily solve?
options: It compresses large payloads before replication.
It allows message recovery when a consumer crashes after taking work.
It guarantees end-to-end exactly-once delivery.
It replaces the need for consumer offsets.
questions.explanation: The queue needs a reclaim path for abandoned work.

questions.question: Which metric most directly shows whether a consumer group is operationally unhealthy?
options: Backlog age and consumer lag by group.
Average topic name length.
The number of YAML files in the deployment repo.
Median producer host uptime.
questions.explanation: Lag and age expose whether work is actually being processed.

questions.question: Why is queue depth alone an incomplete signal?
options: Because depth does not reveal whether the backlog is old, growing, or harmlessly buffered.
Because depth is only measured for push queues.
Because depth becomes meaningless once offsets exist.
Because all healthy queues keep depth at zero.
questions.explanation: Age and trend matter more than a raw count.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with at-least-once delivery?
options: It improves practical recovery at the cost of duplicate-processing risk.
It eliminates the need for retention controls.
It guarantees perfect ordering across all partitions.
It makes poison messages impossible.
questions.explanation: Retries and recovery usually imply possible duplicates.

questions.question: What is the cleanest first mitigation when one poison message keeps blocking a partition?
options: Move the message aside after bounded retries so progress can continue.
Delete the whole topic immediately.
Disable acknowledgements for that consumer group.
Double the replication factor and try again.

questions.question: Why should replay usually be treated as a control-plane operation instead of an unlimited runtime feature?
options: Because large replays can starve live traffic and need quotas or isolation.
Because replays only work in test environments.
Because consumer groups never need historical data.
Because immutable logs cannot be read twice.
questions.explanation: Replay can become its own incident if unconstrained.

questions.question: Which design change best helps a multi-tenant queue survive one very noisy tenant?
options: Add per-tenant quotas and isolation for partitions or throughput.
Remove retention so backlogs disappear faster.
Force every tenant onto one shared partition.
Disable redelivery for all topics.
questions.explanation: Isolation and admission control are the shared-platform answer.
